title: Demographics of Tonga
content:
(table 9.1 of downloadable XL file: "Estimated resident population, Country of birth, State/territory, Age and sex - 30 June 2011")</ref Tongans, a <a href="Polynesians" Polynesian</a group, represent more than 98% of the inhabitants of <a href="Tonga" Tonga</a.

The rest are European (the majority are <a href="British%20people" British</a), mixed European, and other Pacific Islanders. There also are several hundred <a href="Chinese%20in%20Tonga" Chinese</a. Almost two-thirds of the population live on its main island, <a href="Tongatapu" Tongatapu</a.

Although an increasing number of Tongans have moved into the only urban and commercial center, <a href="Nuku%CA%BBalofa" Nukualofa</a, where European and indigenous cultural and living patterns have blended, village life and kinship ties continue to be important throughout the country.

Everyday life is heavily influenced by <a href="Polynesia" Polynesia</a n traditions and especially by the <a href="Christianity" Christian</a faith; for example, all commerce and entertainment activities cease from midnight Saturday until midnight Sunday, and the constitution declares the <a href="Sabbath%20in%20Christianity" Sabbath</a to be sacred, forever.

Other important Christian denominations include <a href="Methodists" Methodists</a (Free Wesleyan) and <a href="Roman%20Catholics" Roman Catholics</a, and <a href="The%20Church%20of%20Jesus%20Christ%20of%20Latter-day%20Saints" The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints</a.

Primary education between ages 6 and 14 is compulsory and free in state schools. Mission schools provide about 83% of the primary and 90% of the secondary level education. Higher education includes teacher training, nursing and medical training, a small private university, a women's business college, and a number of private agricultural schools. Most higher education is pursued overseas.
